source_metadata.title_raw: James R. Williams - A-18-118 - July 19, 2018 - San Jose

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 •. Fax (916) 322-0886 
James R. Williams 
Santa Clara County Counsel 
County Government Center 
70 West Hedding Center 
East Wing, 9th Floor 
San Jose, California 95110 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-118 
Dear Mr. Williams: 
July 19, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Santa Clara County Lead Deputy 
County Counsel Christopher R. Cheleden regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re 
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and 
accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should 
contact us for additional advice. 
QUESTION 
May Deputy County Counsel Christopher R. Cheleden provide legal advice to the County of 
Santa Clara on the Civic Center Master Plan Project where he leases an apartment in the vicinity? 
CONCLUSION 
Mr. Cheleden may not participate in the decision, which includes approval of Phase 1 
construction and demolition, as it will have a reasonably foreseeable and material effect on his 
leasehold interest. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR 
File No. A-18-118 
Page No. 2 
In the next few months, the County will consider the Civic Center Master Plan Project 
("Project"). The Project consists of a 40-acre portion of the 55-acre County of Santa Clara Civic 
Center complex, located in the City of San Jose. The proposed Project is a "Master Plan" for near-
term and long-term redevelopment of the Civic Center site. The site is comprised of four major 
areas made up of twelve parcels located northwest and southwest of the West Hedding Street/North 
First Street intersection. In April 2018, Mr. Cheleden leased an apartment located on Miller Street, 
one block from North First Street and approximately 400 feet from the closest boundary to the 
Civic Center site. Mr. Cheleden has a one-year lease on the $2,300 per-month apartment and is 
prohibited from subleasing his apartment. 
The Master Plan is intended to provide the County with a conceptual roadmap for 
maximizing the site's potential through replacement of many outdated government facilities with 
new buildings designed to consolidate services to the community. In subsequent phone 
conversations and emails requesting additional information, Assistant County Counsel Steve Mitra 
affirmed that the County's May 2018 Project Draft Environmental Impact Report ("Draft EIR") 
reflects the currently-conceived Project.2 As noted in the Draft EIR, the Project seeks to address 
issues related to the County facilities, and also to "promote economic development and enhance the 
Civic Center as a community asset for the neighborhood and North First Street corridor."3 
The County's Draft EIR provides the following information: The Master Plan divides the 
Project into four development sites (A-D). Sites A, B, and C, in the northwest and northeast 
portions of the site are primarily designated for County offices. Site D, in the southeast portion of 
the site, is designated to be corporate offices/campus with associated parking. Mr. Cheleden's 
apartment building is located one block from Site D. The Master Plan proposes Project construction 
in four phases over twenty years, as follows: 
• 
Phase 1: 
o Site A: construction of a Public Safety and Justice Center and associated facilities 
(including a 2,400-space parking garage) at the site of the former Richey Armory at 
the northwest comer of Hedding and San Pedro Streets, with demolition of two 
storage buildings and Richey Armory. 
o Site C: a new plaza and amenity hub of food services and fitness facilities for 
County staff and visitors, new pedestrian crossings, and landscaping. 
2 County's Draft EIR, May 2018, https://www.sccgov.org/sites/faf/capital-
projects/cc/Documents/Draft%20EIR%20Civic%20Center%2004.30. l8.pdf available July 10, 2018. 
Additionally, the Draft EIR states that the Master Plan is consistent with General Plan designations or zoning, 
in that the County's General Plan does not contain policies applicable to construction and operation County-owned 
facilities. County facilities are also not subject to the General Plan Designation and Zoning of the City of San Jose. If 
Site D were to include development of private projects subject to land use approval by the City of San Jose, such 
projects would be subject to applicable policies of the City's General Plan through separate environmental review by 
the City. (Seep. 147.) 
3 Id., "Project Information and Description, Project Objectives" item 2.2.3, p. 7. 


File No. A-18-118 
Page No. 3 
o 
Streets between Sites A, C, and D: The first phase of development includes street 
improvements to be coordinated with the City of San Jose along West Hedding 
Street and North San Pedro Street, a minor realignment of Hedding Street to 
straighten the roadway between North San Pedro Street and North First Street, Street 
improvements could include, but are not limited to, new pedestrian crossings and 
landscaping. 
o Site D: demolition of the six-story Annex building adjacent to the former San Jose City 
Hall to provide a staging area for construction of two potentially overlapping County 
capital facilities projects, including the previously-approved jail replacement facility (at 
the southwest comer of San Pedro and Hedding Streets), and the currently proposed 
Public Safety and Justice Center as well as an approximately 180-space parking lot. A 
two-acre area would be paved for the parking/staging area. A landscape buffer is 
proposed along North First Street and the lawn/landscaping currently located between 
the Annex and North First Street would be maintained. 
• 
Phase 2: develop the western and southern portion of Site C with up to 400,000 square feet 
of office space for Finance and Government operations, including a new Board Chambers 
and logistics hub and a 1,300-space parking structure. 
• 
Phase 3: develop the remaining portion of Site C and the western half of Site B. The eastern 
portion of Site C would be developed with up to 510,000 square feet of office space for the 
Social Services Agency or other County department operations. A 1, 700-space parking 
structure would be constructed on Site B. 
• 
Phase 4: develop the eastern half of Site B and all of Site D. The combined total 
development for this phase would incorporate the existing East Wing building and the 
former San Jose City Hall. It is anticipated that up to 3,100 parking stalls would be required 
to support build-out on Site D, which is estimated to be 1,450,000 square feet, including 
approximately 993,000 square feet of new office space in addition to the existing buildings 
that would remain (east wing of the main County office building and the former San Jose 
City Hall).4 
In your request, you state that with "the exception of the Public Safety and Justice Center, 
approval of the Master Plan will not authorize any construction and it is unknown whether or not 
the County will ultimately approve any construction consistent with the Master Plan." Mr. Mitra 
has confirmed that the Draft EIR's statement of Phase 1 describes a "project" which includes 
construction and demolition involving Sites A, C and D. He noted that Phases 2 through 4 are 
currently at the "programmatic level." As stated in the Draft EIR, Phases 2 through 4 are at a 
conceptual phase and would require additional project-level review prior to proposed development. 5 
4 County's Draft EIR, "Proposed Development- Phase I" through "Future Phases of Development," items 
2.2.6 through 2.2.7.2, pp. 10-15. 
5/d.,p.3 


ANALYSIS 
File No. A-18-118 
Page No. 4 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has an interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, 
within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests distinguishable from the effect on 
the public generally. (Section 87103.) "Financial interests" as defined in Section 87103 and 
implicated by the facts include: 
• 
An interest in any real property where the official has a direct or indirect interest 
worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. (Section 87102(b).)6 
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances. (Section 87103.) 
At issue is whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the County decision to approve the Civic 
Center Master Plan Project will have a material financial effect on Mr. Cheleden's real property 
leasehold interest. 7 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Mr. Cheleden's real property interest lies outside the Civic Center site and is not "explicitly 
involved," as that term is defined in Regulation 18701(a), in the Project decision. The applicable 
standard for determining "foreseeability" is whether a financial effect can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701 (b ). ) 
Regulation 18702.2(b) states that the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
government decision on an official's real property leasehold interest is material if the decision will: 
(1) Change the termination date of the lease; 
(2) Increase or decrease the potential rental value of the property; 
(3) Increase or decrease the rental value of the property, and the official has a 
right to sublease the property; 
(4) Change the official's actual or legally allowable use of the real property; 
(5) Impact the official's use and enjoyment of the real property. 
Mr. Cheleden's apartment, located one block from the North First Street corridor and 400 
feet from the Civic Center site, will be impacted by the Project. You contend that the construction 
6 "Interest in real property" includes any leasehold, exc.ept periodic tenancies of one month or less. (Section 
82033; Regulation 18233.) 
7 We do not analyze the personal financial effect of the decision where a real property interest is involved, and 
the materiality standard set in Regulation 18702.2 is applicable. (Regulation 18702.S(c).) 


File No. A-18-118 
Page No. 5 
of a government building, the Public Safety and Justice Center, is unlikely to change the potential 
rental value of the property in light of the existing governmental uses in the vicinity. However, your 
office confirms that Phase 1 of the Project includes further development. 
Phase 1 also involves demolition and construction-staging activities on Site D, the site 
closest to his apartment. The construction under Phase 1 is estimated to take three to five years to 
complete and may be experienced as a detriment in the neighborhood. Because one of the Project 
objectives is to "promote the economic development and enhance the Civic Center as a community 
asset for the neighborhood and North First Street corridor," the neighborhood will also _experience 
improvements. Mr. Cheleden's apartment is in this neighborhood and stands to benefit from the 
street improvements and amenities designed for the public spaces, and to experience the years of 
near-by demolition and construction-staging. Your office has confirmed that there are no facts 
indicating that Mr. Cheleden's apartment would be buffered from these impacts. 
Based on the facts, it is a realistic possibility that the County's decision to approve the Civic 
Center Master Plan Project may increase or decrease the potential rental value of the property. As a 
result of the demolition and construction, it is also a realistic possibility that the decision may 
impact Mr. Cheleden's use and enjoyment of the real property. Mr. Cheleden has a conflict of 
interest under the Act and may not participate in this decision. 
Making, Participating in Making, or Influencing a Governmental Decision 
Regulation 18704 defines these actions as follows: 
(a) Making a Decision. A public official makes a governmental decision if the 
official authorizes or directs any action, votes, appoints a person, obligates or 
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual 
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. 
(b) Participating in a Decision. A public official participates in a governmental 
decision if the official provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation for 
the purpose of affecting the decision without significant intervening substantive 
review. 
(c) Using Official Position to Attempt to Influence a Decision. A public official 
uses his or her official position to influence a governmental decision if he or she: 
(1) Contacts or appears before any official in his or her agency or in an agency 
subject to the authority or budgetary control of his or her agency for the purpose 
of affecting a decision; or 
(2) Contacts or appears before any official in any other government agency for the 
purpose of affecting a decision, and the public official acts or purports to act 
within his or her authority or on behalf of his or her agency in making the contact. 
Generally, providing legal advice to the County relating to its consideration of the Project 
constitutes "participating in the decision." 


File No. A-18-118 
Page No. 6 
We note that the Master Plan for the Project proposes construction in four phases over 
twenty years, and the decision at issue authorizes only Phase I "at the project level" for 
construction. Regulation 18706 allows that an agency may segment a decision in which a public 
official has a financial interest, to allow participation by the official, provided the following 
conditions are satisfied: 
(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down 
into separate decisions that are not "inextricably interrelated" to the decision in 
which the official has a disqualifying finaQcial interest; 8 
(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from 
the other decisions; 
(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first 
and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official's 
participation in any way; and 
( 4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been made, 
the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening of, or 
otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official was disqualified. 
You have not requested a segmentation analysis. Please contact our office if you wish to 
make a specific proposal for segmentation of the decision, and provide facts sufficient for our office 
to determine if the exception applies. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
LKH:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Acting General Counsel 
By: 
L. Karen Harrison 
Counsel, Legal Division 
8 For purposes of this regulation, decisions are "inextricably interrelated" when the result of one decision will 
effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision. (Regulation I 8706(b ). )

May Deputy County Counsel Christopher R. Cheleden provide legal advice to the County of 
Santa Clara on the Civic Center Master Plan Project where he leases an apartment in the vicinity?

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has an interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, 
within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests distinguishable from the effect on 
the public generally. (Section 87103.) "Financial interests" as defined in Section 87103 and

•

worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. (Section 87102(b).)6 
•

At issue is whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the County decision to approve the Civic 
Center Master Plan Project will have a material financial effect on Mr. Cheleden's real property 
leasehold interest. 7 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Mr. Cheleden's real property interest lies outside the Civic Center site and is not "explicitly 
involved," as that term is defined in Regulation 18701(a), in the Project decision. The applicable 
standard for determining "foreseeability" is whether a financial effect can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701 (b ). ) 
Regulation 18702.2(b) states that the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
government decision on an official's real property leasehold interest is material if the decision will: 
(1) Change the termination date of the lease; 
(2) Increase or decrease the potential rental value of the property; 
(3) Increase or decrease the rental value of the property, and the official has a 
right to sublease the property; 
(4) Change the official's actual or legally allowable use of the real property; 
(5) Impact the official's use and enjoyment of the real property. 
Mr. Cheleden's apartment, located one block from the North First Street corridor and 400 
feet from the Civic Center site, will be impacted by the Project. You contend that the construction 
6 "Interest in real property" includes any leasehold, exc.ept periodic tenancies of one month or less. (Section 
82033; Regulation 18233.) 
7 We do not analyze the personal financial effect of the decision where a real property interest is involved, and 
the materiality standard set in Regulation 18702.2 is applicable. (Regulation 18702.S(c).)  
of a government building, the Public Safety and Justice Center, is unlikely to change the potential 
rental value of the property in light of the existing governmental uses in the vicinity. However, your 
office confirms that Phase 1 of the Project includes further development. 
Phase 1 also involves demolition and construction-staging activities on Site D, the site 
closest to his apartment. The construction under Phase 1 is estimated to take three to five years to 
complete and may be experienced as a detriment in the neighborhood. Because one of the Project 
objectives is to "promote the economic development and enhance the Civic Center as a community 
asset for the neighborhood and North First Street corridor," the neighborhood will also _experience 
improvements. Mr. Cheleden's apartment is in this neighborhood and stands to benefit from the 
street improvements and amenities designed for the public spaces, and to experience the years of 
near-by demolition and construction-staging. Your office has confirmed that there are no facts 
indicating that Mr. Cheleden's apartment would be buffered from these impacts. 
Based on the facts, it is a realistic possibility that the County's decision to approve the Civic 
Center Master Plan Project may increase or decrease the potential rental value of the property. As a 
result of the demolition and construction, it is also a realistic possibility that the decision may 
impact Mr. Cheleden's use and enjoyment of the real property. Mr. Cheleden has a conflict of 
interest under the Act and may not participate in this decision. 
Making, Participating in Making, or Influencing a Governmental Decision 
Regulation 18704 defines these actions as follows: 
(a) Making a Decision. A public official makes a governmental decision if the 
official authorizes or directs any action, votes, appoints a person, obligates or 
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual 
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. 
(b) Participating in a Decision. A public official participates in a governmental 
decision if the official provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation for 
the purpose of affecting the decision without significant intervening substantive 
review. 
(c) Using Official Position to Attempt to Influence a Decision. A public official 
uses his or her official position to influence a governmental decision if he or she: 
(1) Contacts or appears before any official in his or her agency or in an agency 
subject to the authority or budgetary control of his or her agency for the purpose 
of affecting a decision; or 
(2) Contacts or appears before any official in any other government agency for the 
purpose of affecting a decision, and the public official acts or purports to act 
within his or her authority or on behalf of his or her agency in making the contact. 
Generally, providing legal advice to the County relating to its consideration of the Project 
constitutes "participating in the decision."  
We note that the Master Plan for the Project proposes construction in four phases over

conditions are satisfied: 
(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down 
into separate decisions that are not "inextricably interrelated" to the decision in 
which the official has a disqualifying finaQcial interest; 8 
(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from 
the other decisions; 
(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first

the Annex and North First Street would be maintained. 
• 
Phase 2: develop the western and southern portion of Site C with up to 400,000 square feet 
of office space for Finance and Government operations, including a new Board Chambers 
and logistics hub and a 1,300-space parking structure. 
• 
Phase 3: develop the remaining portion of Site C and the western half of Site B. The eastern 
portion of Site C would be developed with up to 510,000 square feet of office space for the 
Social Services Agency or other County department operations. A 1, 700-space parking 
structure would be constructed on Site B. 
• 
Phase 4: develop the eastern half of Site B and all of Site D. The combined total 
development for this phase would incorporate the existing East Wing building and the 
former San Jose City Hall. It is anticipated that up to 3,100 parking stalls would be required 
to support build-out on Site D, which is estimated to be 1,450,000 square feet, including 
approximately 993,000 square feet of new office space in addition to the existing buildings 
that would remain (east wing of the main County office building and the former San Jose 
City Hall).4 
In your request, you state that with "the exception of the Public Safety and Justice Center, 
approval of the Master Plan will not authorize any construction and it is unknown whether or not 
the County will ultimately approve any construction consistent with the Master Plan." Mr. Mitra 
has confirmed that the Draft EIR's statement of Phase 1 describes a "project" which includes 
construction and demolition involving Sites A, C and D. He noted that Phases 2 through 4 are 
currently at the "programmatic level." As stated in the Draft EIR, Phases 2 through 4 are at a 
conceptual phase and would require additional project-level review prior to proposed development. 5 
4 County's Draft EIR, "Proposed Development- Phase I" through "Future Phases of Development," items 
2.2.6 through 2.2.7.2, pp. 10-15. 
5/d.,p.3
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION · 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 •. Fax (916) 322-0886 James R. Williams Santa Clara County Counsel County Government Center 70 West Hedding Center East Wing, 9th Floor San Jose, California 95110 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-18-118 Dear Mr. Williams: July 19, 2018 This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Santa Clara County Lead Deputy County Counsel Christopher R. Cheleden regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. QUESTION May Deputy County Counsel Christopher R. Cheleden provide legal advice to the County of Santa Clara on the Civic Center Master Plan Project where he leases an apartment in the vicinity? CONCLUSION Mr. Cheleden may not participate in the decision, which includes approval of Phase 1 construction and demolition, as it will have a reasonably foreseeable and material effect on his leasehold interest. 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR File No. A-18-118 Page No. 2 In the next few months, the County will consider the Civic Center Master Plan Project ("Project"). The Project consists of a 40-acre portion of the 55-acre County of Santa Clara Civic Center complex, located in the City of San Jose. The proposed Project is a "Master Plan" for near- term and long-term redevelopment of the Civic Center site. The site is comprised of four major areas made up of twelve parcels located northwest and southwest of the West Hedding Street/North First Street intersection. In April 2018, Mr. Cheleden leased an apartment located on Miller Street, one block from North First Street and approximately 400 feet from the closest boundary to the Civic Center site. Mr. Cheleden has a one-year lease on the $2,300 per-month apartment and is prohibited from subleasing his apartment. The Master Plan is intended to provide the County with a conceptual roadmap for maximizing the site's potential through replacement of many outdated government facilities with new buildings designed to consolidate services to the community. In subsequent phone conversations
